title: SEGMENT 6 — Phi29 polymerase: the strand-displacing, high-fidelity, very slow workhorse

lines.text: SEGMENT 6 — Phi29 polymerase: the strand-displacing, high-fidelity, very slow workhorse
We've talked about the chip, the wells, the chemistry.
Now let's spend a segment on the enzyme that sits at the bottom of every well doing the actual work: the polymerase.
Because the choice of polymerase is one of the most consequential decisions in PacBio's whole design, and the enzyme they ended up with has some distinctly weird and useful properties.
The polymerase PacBio uses is derived from one called phi29, often written as Φ29.
Phi29 is the DNA polymerase of a bacteriophage — a virus that infects the bacterium Bacillus subtilis.
Like many bacteriophages, phi29 carries its own DNA polymerase as part of its genome; the phage uses that polymerase to replicate its own genome inside the bacterial host.
The enzyme was originally characterized in the 1980s by the Spanish biochemist Margarita Salas, and it became a workhorse of molecular biology long before PacBio existed, for reasons we'll see.
What makes phi29 special?
First and most important: processivity.
Most DNA polymerases, left to their own devices, will copy a few hundred bases of a template and then fall off.
They're not particularly sticky to the DNA.
To get the polymerase to copy a long stretch, you typically need a partner protein — a "sliding clamp" — that physically rings around the DNA and tethers the polymerase to it.
Phi29 doesn't need that.
It's intrinsically extremely processive.
A phi29 molecule, once it grabs a template, can stay on for tens of thousands of bases — hours of copying — without falling off.
That's exactly what you need for single-molecule sequencing, because there's no way to put a fallen-off polymerase back on the same template; if you lose the enzyme mid-read, the read just stops.
Second: strand displacement activity.

When phi29 is moving along a template and it encounters a stretch of double-stranded DNA — a region where some other piece of DNA is already paired with the template — it doesn't stop and it doesn't fall off.
Instead, it actively pries the other strand off and keeps going, displacing the double-stranded region as it advances.
Imagine a snowplow rolling forward and pushing snow off the road as it moves.
That's strand displacement.
Most polymerases stop at a double-stranded block; phi29 just plows through it.
This property is critical for PacBio's circular templates, which we'll cover in the next segment.

The SMRTbell template is a circular single-stranded DNA loop, and the polymerase reads around it again and again, going past the joint between the original and the copy it just made — which is now double-stranded.
Without strand displacement, the polymerase would stop after one pass.
With strand displacement, it just keeps going.
The polymerase reads around the circle, displacing its own previous copy, around and around, accumulating multiple passes of the same sequence.
This is what enables circular consensus sequencing — the entire HiFi paradigm depends on it.
Third: high fidelity.
Phi29 has a built-in proofreading exonuclease — a "spell-checker" that catches misincorporations and removes them.
So even at the single-molecule level, the per-base error rate of incorporation is fairly low — much lower than, say, a typical Klenow fragment polymerase used in older sequencing chemistry.

The proofreading helps, although it's not perfect; the overall error rate of PacBio raw reads is still in the ten-to-fifteen percent range, but that error comes mostly from the optical detection side — missed pulses, merged pulses, wrong color calls — rather than from the polymerase making chemical mistakes.
Fourth: slow.

Phi29, modified for PacBio chemistry, runs at something like one to ten bases per second — which is glacially slow compared to a normal replicative polymerase that can do hundreds of bases per second.
That slowness is actually good for the platform: it gives the camera enough time per base to integrate a clean signal.
If the polymerase ran at full speed, the pulses would blur together and the optical system couldn't resolve them.
PacBio has tuned the polymerase to a speed where the pulses are well separated and the camera can read them cleanly.
So phi29 ticks every box that PacBio needs.
Highly processive, strand-displacing, high-fidelity, and a workable speed for optical detection.
It's almost as if the enzyme had been designed for the platform — but of course it wasn't.
It was discovered, in nature, in a tiny bacteriophage, doing its job for entirely different reasons.

PacBio's engineers then spent years modifying it to tolerate phospholinked fluorescent nucleotides, to be stable for long runs at room temperature, and to bind well to the streptavidin anchors at the bottom of the ZMW.
The final enzyme PacBio uses is heavily engineered, but its core properties come from the original phi29.
This is, by the way, a recurring pattern in biotechnology — find an enzyme that nature has already optimized for some niche purpose, then re-engineer it for an entirely different application.
Taq polymerase, the enzyme that made PCR possible, came from a thermophilic bacterium living in hot springs.
The Cas9 enzyme of CRISPR came from a bacterial immune system.
Phi29 came from a bacteriophage.
In each case, the discovery of the enzyme came first; the application came years or decades later.

It's worth remembering that the foundational molecular tools of the biotech revolution were almost all gifts from microbial evolution — sitting in libraries of obscure enzymes, waiting to be repurposed.
One last note about the polymerase.

PacBio has developed several generations of polymerase chemistry, named by version — the C2, P4, P5, P6 chemistries on the original RS platform, the various Sequel chemistries, and the most recent versions on Revio.

Each new chemistry has typically delivered some combination of: longer reads (because the polymerase stays on longer), higher accuracy (because of better fidelity or better dye discrimination), or higher throughput (because of better well loading or faster cycling).
The chemistry versions are part of why you'll see PacBio papers from different years reporting very different read lengths and accuracies.
Same instrument, same fundamental physics, different chemistry generation.
The platform has been improving steadily for over a decade.
Now, with the polymerase in hand, let's talk about the template — the SMRTbell — because the SMRTbell is the design innovation that turns PacBio into a self-error-correcting sequencer.
